title: Paradise Lost - Book IX
content:
آ آ آ آ Meanwhile the hainous and despightfull act
آ آ آ آ Of Satan done in Paradise, and how
آ آ آ آ Hee in the Serpent had perverted Eve,
آ آ آ آ Her Husband shee, to taste the fatall fruit,
آ آ آ آ Was known in Heavï؟½n; for what can scape the Eye
آ آ آ آ Of God All-seeing, or deceave his Heart
آ آ آ آ Omniscient, who in all things wise and just,
آ آ آ آ Hinderï؟½d not Satan to attempt the minde
آ آ آ آ Of Man, with strength entire, and free Will armï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Complete to have discoverï؟½d and repulst
آ آ آ آ Whatever wiles of Foe or seeming Friend.
آ آ آ آ For still they knew, and ought to have still rememberï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The high Injunction not to taste that Fruit,
آ آ آ آ Whoever tempted; which they not obeying,
آ آ آ آ Incurrï؟½d, what could they less, the penaltie,
آ آ آ آ And manifold in sin, deservï؟½d to fall.
آ آ آ آ Up into Heavï؟½n from Paradise in hast
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ Angelic Guards ascended, mute and sad
آ آ آ آ For Man, for of his state by this they knew,
آ آ آ آ Much wondring how the suttle Fiend had stoln
آ آ آ آ Entrance unseen. Soon as thï؟½ unwelcome news
آ آ آ آ From Earth arrivï؟½d at Heaven Gate, displeasï؟½d
آ آ آ آ All were who heard, dim sadness did not spare
آ آ آ آ That time Celestial visages, yet mixt
آ آ آ آ With pitie, violated not thir bliss.
آ آ آ آ About the new-arrivï؟½d, in multitudes
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ ethereal People ran, to hear and know
آ آ آ آ How all befell: they towards the Throne Supream
آ آ آ آ Accountable made haste to make appear
آ آ آ آ With righteous plea, thir utmost vigilance,
آ آ آ آ And easily approvï؟½d; when the most High
آ آ آ آ Eternal Father from his secret Cloud,
آ آ آ آ Amidst in Thunder utterï؟½d thus his voice.
آ آ آ آ Assemblï؟½d Angels, and ye Powers returnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ From unsuccessful charge, be not dismaid,
آ آ آ آ Nor troublï؟½d at these tidings from the Earth,
آ آ آ آ Which your sincerest care could not prevent,
آ آ آ آ Foretold so lately what would come to pass,
آ آ آ آ When first this Tempter crossï؟½d the Gulf from Hell.
آ آ آ آ I told ye then he should prevail and speed
آ آ آ آ On his bad Errand, Man should be seducï؟½t
آ آ آ آ And flatterï؟½d out of all, believing lies
آ آ آ آ Against his Maker; no Decree of mine
آ آ آ آ Concurring to necessitate his Fall,
آ آ آ آ Or touch with lightest moment of impulse
آ آ آ آ His free Will, to her own inclining left
آ آ آ آ In eevn scale. But fallï؟½n he is, and now
آ آ آ آ What rests, but that the mortal Sentence pass
آ آ آ آ On his transgression, Death denouncï؟½t that day,
آ آ آ آ Which he presumes already vain and void,
آ آ آ آ Because not yet inflicted, as he fearï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ By some immediate stroak; but soon shall find
آ آ آ آ Forbearance no acquittance ere day end.
آ آ آ آ Justice shall not return as bountie scornï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ But whom send I to judge them? whom but thee
آ آ آ آ Vicegerent Son, to thee I have transferrï؟½d
آ آ آ آ All Judgement, whether in Heavï؟½n, or Earth; or Hell.
آ آ آ آ Easie it may be seen that I intend
آ آ آ آ Mercie collegue with Justice, sending thee
آ آ آ آ Mans Friend, his Mediator, his designï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Both Ransom and Redeemer voluntarie,
آ آ آ آ And destinï؟½d Man himself to judge Man fallï؟½n.
آ آ آ آ So spake the Father, and unfoulding bright
آ آ آ آ Toward the right hand his Glorie, on the Son
آ آ آ آ Blazï؟½d forth unclouded Deitie; he full
آ آ آ آ Resplendent all his Father manifest
آ آ آ آ Expressï؟½d, and thus divinely answerï؟½d milde.
آ آ آ آ Father Eternal, thine is to decree,
آ آ آ آ Mine both in Heavï؟½n and Earth to do thy will
آ آ آ آ Supream, that thou in mee thy Son belovï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Mayst ever rest well pleasï؟½d. I go to judge
آ آ آ آ On Earth these thy transgressors, but thou knowst,
آ آ آ آ Whoever judgï؟½d, the worst on mee must light,
آ آ آ آ When time shall be, for so I undertook
آ آ آ آ Before thee; and not repenting, this obtaine
آ آ آ آ Of right, that I may mitigate thir doom
آ آ آ آ On me derivï؟½d, yet I shall temper so
آ آ آ آ Justice with Mercie, as may illustrate most
آ آ آ آ Them fully satisfied, and thee appease.
آ آ آ آ Attendance none shall need, nor Train, where none
آ آ آ آ Are to behold the Judgement, but the judgï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Those two; the third best absent is condemnï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Convict by flight, and Rebel to all Law
آ آ آ آ Conviction to the Serpent none belongs.
آ آ آ آ Thus saying, from his radiant Seat he rose
آ آ آ آ Of high collateral glorie: him Thrones and Powers,
آ آ آ آ Princedoms, and Dominations ministrant
آ آ آ آ Accompanied to Heaven Gate, from whence
آ آ آ آ Eden and all the Coast in prospect lay.
آ آ آ آ Down he descended strait; the speed of Gods
آ آ آ آ Time counts not, though with swiftest minutes wingï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Now was the Sun in Western cadence low
آ آ آ آ From Noon, and gentle Aires due at thir hour
آ آ آ آ To fan the Earth now wakï؟½d, and usher in
آ آ آ آ The Eevning coole when he from wrauth more coole
آ آ آ آ Came the mild Judge and Intercessor both
آ آ آ آ To sentence Man: the voice of God they heard
آ آ آ آ Now walking in the Garden, by soft windes
آ آ آ آ Brought to thir Ears, while day declinï؟½d, they heard
آ آ آ آ And from his presence hid themselves among
آ آ آ آ The thickest Trees, both Man and Wife, till God
آ آ آ آ Approaching, thus to Adam callï؟½d aloud.
آ آ آ آ Where art thou Adam, wont with joy to meet
آ آ آ آ My coming seen far off? I miss thee here,
آ آ آ آ Not pleasï؟½d, thus entertaind with solitude,
آ آ آ آ Where obvious dutie erewhile appearï؟½d unsaught:
آ آ آ آ Or come I less conspicuous, or what change
آ آ آ آ Absents thee, or what chance detains? Come forth.
آ آ آ آ He came, and with him Eve, more loth, though first
آ آ آ آ To offend, discountï؟½nancï؟½t both, and discomposï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Love was not in thir looks, either to God
آ آ آ آ Or to each other, but apparent guilt,
آ آ آ آ And shame, and perturbation, and despaire,
آ آ آ آ Anger, and obstinacie, and hate, and guile.
آ آ آ آ Whence Adam faultring long, thus answerï؟½d brief.
آ آ آ آ I heard thee in the Garden, and of thy voice
آ آ آ آ Affraid, being naked, hid my self. To whom
آ آ آ آ The gracious Judge without revile repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ My voice thou oft hast heard, and hast not fearï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ But still rejoycï؟½t, how is it now become
آ آ آ آ So dreadful to thee? that thou art naked, who
آ آ آ آ Hath told thee? hast thou eaten of the Tree
آ آ آ آ Whereof I gave thee charge thou shouldst not eat?
آ آ آ آ To whom thus Adam sore beset repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ O Heavï؟½n! in evil strait this day I stand
آ آ آ آ Before my Judge, either to undergoe
آ آ آ آ My self the total Crime, or to accuse
آ آ آ آ My other self, the partner of my life;
آ آ آ آ Whose failing, while her Faith to me remaines,
آ آ آ آ I should conceal, and not expose to blame
آ آ آ آ By my complaint; but strict necessitie
آ آ آ آ Subdues me, and calamitous constraint,
آ آ آ آ Least on my head both sin and punishment,
آ آ آ آ However insupportable, be all
آ آ آ آ Devolvï؟½d; though should I hold my peace, yet thou
آ آ آ آ Wouldst easily detect what I conceale.
آ آ آ آ This Woman whom thou madï؟½st to be my help,
آ آ آ آ And gavï؟½st me as thy perfet gift, so good,
آ آ آ آ So fit, so acceptable, so Divine,
آ آ آ آ That from her hand I could suspect no ill,
آ آ آ آ And what she did, whatever in it self,
آ آ آ آ Her doing seemï؟½d to justifie the deed;
آ آ آ آ Shee gave me of the Tree, and I did eate.
آ آ آ آ To whom the sovran Presence thus repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Was shee thy God, that her thou didst obey
آ آ آ آ Before his voice, or was shee made thy guide,
آ آ آ آ Superior, or but equal, that to her
آ آ آ آ Thou didï؟½st resigne thy Manhood, and the Place
آ آ آ آ Wherein God set thee above her made of thee,
آ آ آ آ And for thee, whose perfection farr excellï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Hers in all real dignitie: Adornd
آ آ آ آ She was indeed, and lovely to attract
آ آ آ آ Thy Love, not thy Subjection, and her Gifts
آ آ آ آ Were such as under Government well seemï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Unseemly to beare rule, which was thy part
آ آ آ آ And person, hadï؟½st thou known thy self aright.
آ آ آ آ So having said, he thus to Eve in few:
آ آ آ آ Say Woman, what is this which thou hast done?
آ آ آ آ To whom sad Eve with shame nigh overwhelmï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Confessing soon, yet not before her Judge
آ آ آ آ Bold or loquacious, thus abasht repliï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ The Serpent me beguilï؟½d and I did eate.
آ آ آ آ Which when the Lord God heard, without delay
آ آ آ آ To Judgement he proceeded on thï؟½ accusï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Serpent though brute, unable to transferre
آ آ آ آ The Guilt on him who made him instrument
آ آ آ آ Of mischief, and polluted from the end
آ آ آ آ Of his Creation; justly then accurst,
آ آ آ آ As vitiated in Nature: more to know
آ آ آ آ Concernï؟½d not Man (since he no further knew)
آ آ آ آ Nor alterï؟½d his offence; yet God at last
آ آ آ آ To Satan first in sin his doom applyï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Though in mysterious terms, judgï؟½d as then best:
آ آ آ آ And on the Serpent thus his curse let fall.
آ آ آ آ Because thou hast done this, thou art accurst
آ آ آ آ Above all Cattel, each Beast of the Field;
آ آ آ آ Upon thy Belly groveling thou shalt goe,
آ آ آ آ And dust shalt eat all the days of thy Life.
آ آ آ آ Between Thee and the Woman I will put
آ آ آ آ Enmitie, and between thine and her Seed;
آ آ آ آ Her Seed shall bruise thy head, thou bruise his heel.
آ آ آ آ So spake this Oracle, then verifiï؟½d
آ آ آ آ When Jesus son of Mary second Eve,
آ آ آ آ Saw Satan fall like Lightning down from Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ Prince of the Aire; then rising from his Grave
آ آ آ آ Spoild Principalities and Powers, triumpht
آ آ آ آ In open shew, and with ascention bright
آ آ آ آ Captivity led captive through the Aire,
آ آ آ آ The Realme it self of Satan long usurpt,
آ آ آ آ Whom he shall tread at last under our feet;
آ آ آ آ Eevn hee who now foretold his fatal bruise,
آ آ آ آ And to the Woman thus his Sentence turnï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Thy sorrow I will greatly multiplie
آ آ آ آ By thy Conception; Children thou shalt bring
آ آ آ آ In sorrow forth, and to thy Husbands will
آ آ آ آ Thine shall submit, hee over thee shall rule.
آ آ آ آ On Adam last thus judgement he pronouncï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Because thou hast hearkï؟½nd to the voice of thy Wife,
آ آ آ آ And eaten of the Tree concerning which
آ آ آ آ I chargï؟½d thee, saying: Thou shalt not eate thereof,
آ آ آ آ Cursï؟½d is the ground for thy sake, thou in sorrow
آ آ آ آ Shalt eate thereof all the days of thy Life;
آ آ آ آ Thornes also and Thistles it shall bring thee forth
آ آ آ آ Unbid, and thou shalt eate thï؟½ Herb of thï؟½ Field,
آ آ آ آ In the sweat of thy Face shalt thou eate Bread,
آ آ آ آ Till thou return unto the ground, for thou
آ آ آ آ Out of the ground wast taken, know thy Birth,
آ آ آ آ For dust thou art, and shalt to dust returne.
آ آ آ آ So judgï؟½d he Man, both Judge and Saviour sent,
آ آ آ آ And thï؟½ instant stroke of Death denouncï؟½t that day
آ آ آ آ Removï؟½d farr off; then pittying how they stood
آ آ آ آ Before him naked to the aire, that now
آ آ آ آ Must suffer change, disdainï؟½d not to begin
آ آ آ آ Thenceforth the forme of servant to assume,
آ آ آ آ As when he washï؟½d his servants feet, so now
آ آ آ آ As Father of his Familie he clad
آ آ آ آ Thir nakedness with Skins of Beasts, or slain,
آ آ آ آ Or as the Snake with youthful Coate repaid;
آ آ آ آ And thought not much to cloath his Enemies:
آ آ آ آ Nor hee thir outward onely with the Skins
آ آ آ آ Of Beasts, but inward nakedness, much more
آ آ آ آ Opprobrious, with his Robe of righteousness,
آ آ آ آ Araying coverï؟½d from his Fathers sight.
آ آ آ آ To him with swift ascent he up returnd,
آ آ آ آ Into his blissful bosom reassumï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In glory as of old, to him appeasï؟½d
آ آ آ آ All, though all-knowing, what had past with Man
آ آ آ آ Recounted, mixing intercession sweet.
آ آ آ آ Meanwhile ere thus was sinï؟½d and judgï؟½d on Earth,
آ آ آ آ Within the Gates of Hell sate Sin and Death,
آ آ آ آ In counterview within the Gates, that now
آ آ آ آ Stood open wide, belching outrageous flame
آ آ آ آ Farr into Chaos, since the Fiend passï؟½d through,
آ آ آ آ Sin opening, who thus now to Death began.
آ آ آ آ O Son, why sit we here each other viewing
آ آ آ آ Idlely, while Satan our great Author thrives
آ آ آ آ In other Worlds, and happier Seat provides
آ آ آ آ For us his ofspring deare? It cannot be
آ آ آ آ But that success attends him; if mishap,
آ آ آ آ Ere this he had returnï؟½d, with fury drivï؟½n
آ آ آ آ By his Avenger, since no place like this
آ آ آ آ Can fit his punishment, or their revenge.
آ آ آ آ Methinks I feel new strength within me rise,
آ آ آ آ Wings growing, and Dominion givï؟½n me large
آ آ آ آ Beyond this Deep; whatever drawes me on,
آ آ آ آ Or sympathie, or som connatural force
آ آ آ آ Powerful at greatest distance to unite
آ آ آ آ With secret amity things of like kinde
آ آ آ آ By secretest conveyance. Thou my Shade
آ آ آ آ Inseparable must with mee along:
آ آ آ آ For Death from Sin no power can separate.
آ آ آ آ But least the difficultie of passing back
آ آ آ آ Stay his returne perhaps over this Gulfe
آ آ آ آ Impassable, impervious, let us try
آ آ آ آ Adventrous work, yet to thy power and mine
آ آ آ آ Not unagreeable, to found a path
آ آ آ آ Over this Maine from Hell to that new World
آ آ آ آ Where Satan now prevailes, a Monument
آ آ آ آ Of merit high to all thï؟½ infernal Host,
آ آ آ آ Easing thir passage hence, for intercourse,
آ آ آ آ Or transmigration, as thir lot shall lead.
آ آ آ آ Nor can I miss the way, so strongly drawn
آ آ آ آ By this new felt attraction and instinct.
آ آ آ آ Whom thus the meager Shadow answerd soon.
آ آ آ آ Goe whither Fate and inclination strong
آ آ آ آ Leads thee, I shall not lag behinde, nor erre
آ آ آ آ The way, thou leading, such a sent I draw
آ آ آ آ Of carnage, prey innumerable, and taste
آ آ آ آ The savour of Death from all things there that live:
آ آ آ آ Nor shall I to the work thou enterprisest
آ آ آ آ Be wanting, but afford thee equal aid.
آ آ آ آ So saying, with delight he snuffï؟½d the smell
آ آ آ آ Of mortal change on Earth. As when a flock
آ آ آ آ Of ravenous Fowl, though many a League remote,
آ آ آ آ Against the day of Battel, to a Field,
آ آ آ آ Where Armies lie encampt, come flying, lurï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With sent of living Carcasses designï؟½d
آ آ آ آ For death, the following day, in bloodie fight.
آ آ آ آ So sented the grim Feature, and upturnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His Nostril wide into the murkie Air,
آ آ آ آ Sagacious of his Quarrey from so farr.
آ آ آ آ Then Both from out Hell Gates into the waste
آ آ آ آ Wide Anarchie of Chaos damp and dark
آ آ آ آ Flew divers, and with Power (thir Power was great)
آ آ آ آ Hovering upon the Waters; what they met
آ آ آ آ Solid or slimie, as in raging Sea
آ آ آ آ Tost up and down, together crowded drove
آ آ آ آ From each side shoaling towards the mouth of Hell.
آ آ آ آ As when two Polar Winds blowing adverse
آ آ آ آ Upon the Cronian Sea, together drive
آ آ آ آ Mountains of Ice, that stop thï؟½ imaginï؟½d way
آ آ آ آ Beyond Petsora Eastward, to the rich
آ آ آ آ Cathaian Coast. The aggregated Soyle
آ آ آ آ Death with his Mace petrific, cold and dry,
آ آ آ آ As with a Trident smote, and fixï؟½t as firm
آ آ آ آ As Delos floating once; the rest his look
آ آ آ آ Bound with Gorgonian rigor not to move,
آ آ آ آ And with Asphaltic slime; broad as the Gate,
آ آ آ آ Deep to the Roots of Hell the gatherï؟½d beach
آ آ آ آ They fastenï؟½d, and the Mole immense wraught on
آ آ آ آ Over the foaming deep high Archt, a Bridge
آ آ آ آ Of length prodigious joyning to the Wall
آ آ آ آ Immoveable of this now fenceless world
آ آ آ آ Forfeit to Death; from hence a passage broad,
آ آ آ آ Smooth, easie, inoffensive down to Hell.
آ آ آ آ So, if great things to small may be comparï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Xerxes, the Libertie of Greece to yoke,
آ آ آ آ From Susa his Memnonian Palace high
آ آ آ آ Came to the Sea, and over Hellespont
آ آ آ آ Bridging his way, Europe with Asia joynï؟½d,

meaning: About: John Milton was an English poet, prose polemicist, and civil servant for the Commonwealth of England. Most famed for his epic poem Paradise Lost, Milton is celebrated as well for his treatise condemning censorship, Areopagitica. Long considered the supreme English poet, Milton experienced a dip in popularity after attacks by T. S. Eliot and F. R. Leavis in the mid 20th century; but with multiple societies and scholarly journals devoted to his study, Miltonï؟½s reputation remains as strong as ever in the 21st century.
